What we treat

Services & specialties

From musculoskeletal and orthopedic conditions to neurologic and pediatric cases, our licensed team treats a wide range of conditions with coordinated PT and OT programs.

Physical Therapy

Musculoskeletal & orthopedic

Fracture Total/partial hip replacement Frozen shoulder Arthritis Hip, knee & back pain Scoliosis Generalized body weakness Myofascial pain syndrome
PT + OT

Neurologic cases

Bell's palsy Stroke Spinal cord injury Traumatic brain injury Parkinson's disease / PSP Multiple sclerosis Guillain-Barré syndrome Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
Occupational Therapy

Pediatric cases

Cerebral palsy Global developmental delay
Where it hurts

Explore what we treat, area by area

Tap a point on the body to see the chronic conditions and pain we help resolve in that region.

1 Head & neck

Neck & cervical spine

Neck pain and headaches often stem from posture, disc irritation, or muscle tension. We restore mobility and calm the nerves that refer pain into the head and arms.

How it works

A clear path from
injury to independence

Every plan is built around your goals and reviewed as you progress. No cookie-cutter protocols — just measurable steps forward.

Start with an evaluation
01
Evaluation & diagnosis
A full movement and functional assessment to understand exactly what's holding you back.
02
Personalized plan
A treatment roadmap combining PT and OT, tailored to your body, schedule, and goals.
03
Hands-on treatment
Guided sessions with manual therapy, exercise, and real-time coaching to rebuild function.
04
Progress & discharge
Measured re-assessments and a home program so gains last well beyond your final visit.

Common questions

What's the difference between physical therapy and occupational therapy?

Physical therapy (PT) focuses on restoring movement, strength, and physical function — walking, balance, mobility. Occupational therapy (OT) focuses on the skills needed for daily life and independence — dressing, cooking, writing, work tasks, fine motor control. Many patients receive both, especially after stroke, surgery, or injury.

How long does each session last?

Most PT and OT sessions run 45–60 minutes, though initial evaluations may take slightly longer.

How many sessions will I need?

This varies by condition and severity, but many patients see noticeable progress within 6–12 sessions. Your therapist will give you a more specific estimate after your evaluation.

At what age can a child start occupational therapy?

OT can begin in infancy if developmental concerns are identified, though it's most commonly started in toddler and early school years for delays in motor skills, sensory processing, or behavior.

Do you treat older adults differently than younger patients?

Yes. Geriatric rehabilitation places extra emphasis on balance, fall prevention, and functional independence, with treatment paced according to the patient's overall health and mobility.

Do you offer therapy for post-surgical recovery?

Yes, we support recovery following a range of procedures, coordinating closely with your surgeon's timeline and guidelines.

Is therapy safe for chronic or progressive conditions?

Yes. Treatment plans are carefully tailored to your specific diagnosis and current abilities, with intensity and technique adjusted to what's safe and effective for your condition.
